perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information of the Furnishing Party will be kept confidential by the Receiving Party and its Representatives and, except with the specific prior written consent of the Furnishing Party, or as expressly otherwise permitted by the terms hereof, will not be disclosed by the Receiving Party or its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information of the Furnishing Party will be kept reasonably confidential by the Receiving Party and its Representatives and, except with the specific prior written consent of the Furnishing Party, or as expressly otherwise permitted by the terms hereof, will not be unnecessarily disclosed by the Receiving Party or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'kept confidential' to 'kept reasonably confidential' and 'will not be disclosed' to 'will not be unnecessarily disclosed' introduces ambiguity. 'Reasonably' and 'unnecessarily' are subjective and create a loophole, potentially allowing for breaches of confidentiality under the guise of reasonable judgment or perceived necessity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes unauthorized disclosure. The modified text allows for subjective interpretation of what is 'reasonably confidential' and 'unnecessarily disclosed', which weakens the protection of confidential information guaranteed by CUTSA. The statute aims to prevent any unauthorized disclosure, not just unreasonable or unnecessary ones.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'kept confidential' to 'kept reasonably confidential' and 'will not be disclosed' to 'will not be unnecessarily disclosed.' This weakens the protection of confidential information because 'reasonably' and 'unnecessarily' are subjective, creating potential loopholes for breaches. The scraped law snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. By allowing for 'reasonable' breaches, the changed text contradicts the law's aim to prevent unauthorized disclosure, regardless of whether it's deemed 'unnecessary' or 'unreasonable'. The original contract ensures a higher standard of confidentiality, whereas the changed contract's subjective terms potentially conflict with the statute's objective to protect trade secrets from unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall limit disclosure of Highly Confidential Information to Company employees. Company shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any such -1- <PAGE> employee. Company shall not disclose Highly Confidential Information to any of its consultants, or other vendors, or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: Company should aim to limit disclosure of Highly Confidential Information to Company employees to the best of its ability. Company may be responsible for breaches of this Agreement by such employees depending on the circumstances. Company will generally try not to disclose Highly Confidential Information to its consultants, or other vendors, or agents.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall limit' to 'should aim to limit to the best of its ability,' 'shall be responsible' to 'may be responsible depending on the circumstances,' and 'shall not disclose' to 'will generally try not to disclose' significantly weakens the obligations regarding the handling of Highly Confidential Information. This introduces subjectivity and makes enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By making the obligation to limit disclosure and prevent breaches less definitive ('aim to limit', 'may be responsible', 'generally try not to disclose'), the modified text falls short of the reasonable measures required by CUTSA, potentially allowing for misappropriation due to lax security.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original contract's obligation to protect confidential information. The original text uses "shall" and "shall not," creating a definitive obligation. The changed text replaces these with "should aim to," "may be responsible," and "will generally try not to," introducing ambiguity and weakening the commitment to protect confidential information. The LLM Law Explanation mentions CUTSA (presumably the Uniform Trade Secrets Act) requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' and includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the obligation to maintain secrecy, the changed text arguably reduces the 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction. The original contract creates a strong obligation, while the changed text creates a weaker, less enforceable obligation.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives are requested or required by applicable Federal or State law of the United States to disclose any of the Confidential Information, it is agreed that the Receiving Party or its Representative, as the case may be, will provide the Furnishing Party with prompt notice of such request(s) so that it may seek an appropriate protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance by the Receiving Party or its Representative with the provisions of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives are requested by applicable Federal or State law of the United States to disclose any of the Confidential Information, it is agreed that the Receiving Party or its Representative, as the case may be, will endeavor to provide the Furnishing Party with notice of such request(s) so that it may consider seeking a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text changes 'are requested or required' to 'are requested,' removing the mandatory aspect of a legal requirement to disclose. It also changes 'will provide prompt notice' to 'will endeavor to provide notice' and 'seek an appropriate protective order' to 'consider seeking a protective order,' making the notification and protective action discretionary rather than obligatory.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 45 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure governs subpoenas, which can legally compel the disclosure of information. If a subpoena requires disclosure of confidential information, the original text mandates prompt notice to the Furnishing Party. The modified text weakens this obligation by only requiring an 'endeavor' to provide notice and 'considering' seeking a protective order, potentially hindering the Furnishing Party's ability to protect its confidential information when legally compelled by a subpoena, therefore, the receiving party does not need to immediately inform the furnishing party about the request for the information, thus going against what the federal laws expect of them.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to provide notice and seek a protective order, potentially hindering the Furnishing Party's ability to protect its confidential information. While the scraped snippets do not directly address the specifics of 'prompt notice' or 'endeavor to provide notice' in the context of confidential information disclosure, the Law Explanation's mention of Rule 45 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure highlights the legal power of subpoenas to compel disclosure. The change from 'will provide prompt notice' to 'will endeavor to provide notice' and 'seek an appropriate protective order' to 'consider seeking a protective order' introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original contract mandated specific actions, potentially resulting in non-compliance with legal expectations.

perturbation.original_text: Upon providing the Receiving Party with a specific list of the Confidential Information to be returned to the Furnishing Party, the Receiving Party will promptly deliver to the Furnishing Party or its Representatives, the Confidential Information listed, together with all copies thereof, in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Representatives, which was actually furnished by the Furnishing Party to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon providing the Receiving Party with a general idea of the Confidential Information to be returned to the Furnishing Party, the Receiving Party will try to deliver to the Furnishing Party or its Representatives, some of the Confidential Information, and maybe copies, in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Representatives, that the Receiving Party remembers receiving from the Furnishing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces several ambiguities: 'specific list' becomes 'general idea', 'will promptly deliver' becomes 'will try to deliver', 'all copies thereof' becomes 'maybe copies,' and 'which was actually furnished' becomes 'that the Receiving Party remembers receiving'. This significantly weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to return confidential information, creating loopholes for non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA aims to prevent misappropriation, and reasonable efforts to return trade secrets are vital in preventing further harm. The modified text, by weakening the obligation to return all confidential information and copies, reduces the Receiving Party's accountability and could result in continued misappropriation, contradicting the Act's intention to protect trade secrets through full return of materials. The party can claim they 'forgot' or 'didn't realize it was confidential information'.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces significant ambiguities weakening the obligation to return confidential information, potentially resulting in continued misappropriation, conflicting with CUTSA's intention to protect trade secrets, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text requires a 'specific list' and 'prompt delivery' of 'all copies', while the changed text uses phrases like 'general idea', 'try to deliver', and 'maybe copies', creating loopholes and reducing accountability. According to California Code, CIV 3426.1, 'Misappropriation' includes the 'use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By weakening the return obligations, the modified text increases the risk of such unauthorized use, thereby contradicting the legal intention to prevent misappropriation.
